entries.title: 87 Years
entries.body: Something happened then. No one knows exactly what. The world was plunged into darkness, as all the gods and goddesses simply disappeared. Have they left? Have they died? Have they lost their powers? What we do know is that the world shattered. The land was torn apart, the skies grew dark and stormy and the seas rised up, swallowing more than 80% of everything.

entries.title: New World, New Era
entries.body: And so the only land left we knew of was this. A single continent, named after the world itself: Iari'sios. The world was forever changed, and so were we. We had to adapt to survive, and we did. We built new homes and new villages. We learned to live with the darkness, the storms, the monsters that now roamed the land. We learned to fight back, to protect ourselves, to thrive in this new world.

entries.title: Spreading Corruption
entries.body: Something no one could even imagine back then was that the calamity, or the lack of divinity, would cause a new kind of corruption to spread accross the land. A corruption that would twist and mutate everything it touched, creating new monsters and new dangers. Nothing can truly stop it... Or at least, nothing that we, almost one hundread years later, know of.

entries.title: Dangers of the Wilds
entries.body: The world outside settlements is a dangerous place now. Especially the monstrosities the Umbra has created. Those who die out there are often found roaming the lands as mindless husks, or worse, as twisted and mutated creatures that attack anything that moves. No single Umbra-Touched is like the others, each unique to its own way. And you would do well to steer clear of them, if you find yourself near one. We don't want to use more energy or resources than needed to fight head on against these things, so we usually just lure them away from our settlements and let them roam the wilds or simply leave them be. And this is only done in groups, never alone. We don't have, not even a single instance, of someone surviving an encounter with an Umbra-Touched alone.

entries.title: The Settlements' Rise
entries.body: But... Not everything is just darkness and despair. We have built new homes, new defenses. We have even found love and happiness in this new world. We truly are a resilient people, with strong will to survive. And we will continue to strive to make this world a better place, even in the face of such adversity. Who knows... Maybe one day we will find a way to bring back the gods, or to stop this madness. Maybe you will be the one to do it... We can only hope.

entries.title: Protector's Crystal
entries.body: Strange crystals that have been found by the explorers deep in the wilds, and have been named 'Protector's Crystal' by our scholars. They are said to have some sort of reminiscence of the divine, and are believed to have some sort of protective properties. They are extremely rare and as such, must be protected at all costs. They are used to create the central spires of each settlement: the tallest and usually central building of each village. The light emmited by them is the only thing capable of keeping the monstrosities away, since not even fire can stop them. Some people say they were the last gift of the gods before they left.

entries.title: Umbral Storm
entries.body: Some twenty years after the calamity, the first Umbral Storm was recorded. A storm unlike any other, with dark clouds capable of swallowing any light other than the Crystal's. The wind sounds like screams and the rain is acidic, able to burn through skin and any organic material. If you ever find yourself far from the protection of the Light and an Umbral Storm is approaching... Run. Don't look back, just run. No one knows what causes it, but some scholars thing it's the Umbra itself changing... adapting.

entries.title: Lost Hope
entries.body: Whenever life is lost, we do not bury them, we burn them. One of the properties of the darkness is that it can reanimate the dead, and so we have to make sure that those who pass on do not come back as something worse.
